author: @mkhamlach
text:
Hi Rob, that s a really interesting cell. I read the patent and that s fascinating.
I think a lot of concept turning around this karpen cell...crystal battery, your capacitor with two types of carbon....it seems to be very important to understand.
I am testing it and no surprise it really works.
I though to do a dry karpen cell with an solid electrolyte like a paint electrolyte or o sort of gel. But it doesn t give me the same results with the original concept.
I am tellling that s may be impossible to do a karpen cell....could you give your point if view about a dry karpen cell.
Do I lose my time?
Thanks

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Mo Kha I think it is to do with transition between phases - so if it is dry then it probably won't work - but that is only what i think mate and i could well be wrong

replies.author: @mkhamlach
replies.text: Thanks for you opinion. I think the best alternative is a gel electrolyte. I read the agar gel is used for this kind of application. Have to test it.

author: @actudoran
text:
let me congratulate you first for the quality of your video!
I live in Romania and have seen the Karpen pile myself exposed at the technical museum in Bucharest.
great example of how it can be done! I will replicate myself soon but with backyard equipment ... I do not have access to lab level equipment. Still on a theoretical basis ... if the cell gets its energy from surrounding "heat", would it make more energy if say exposed to sun or in a warmer room ? (ok I know on some level the heightened entropy helps the electrical charges get around quicker, but I meant can we detect a bigger delta between the two states than a directly proportional temp increase? if at all ? )

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - i would love to have seen it - i did think of visiting but i am guessing you need to make an appointment. I would guess it would respond exactly the way you suggest but this is why we experiment to answer these questions

I was wondering if you could give me a tip or two for my replication ? I basically need to replicate the amilic alcohol NaOH mixture or any of the other 2 variants on the patent but it has to be relevant for my audience :)
hence it has to be easy to replicate with basic materials ( I know graphene is totally awsome but still ... $$$ ) that are easy to procure ... like sodium bicarbonate can be dehidrated to NaOH in the oven for a while ... and have to be scalable but that's the easy bit ...

any clues ? ( I still am going through the youtube trying to see all your excelent posts )

cheers

author: @KimberlyRPeacock
text: A little bit of a rant aimed at critics.  In science you should have healthy scepticism, and ask question, and out them to experimental test. However there are way too many individuals who think they already know everything there is to know and so they handwave saying things like the Karpin Cell is BS, or that the Oxford Bell is just a slow reacting battery.  These are the same people who will deride Senft's work on low temperature differential Stirling engines. They will say but they are not practical. It's like they are saying nothing to learn, or see, or hear, here, please look away. Well Ladies and Gentleman that is utter rubbish!   While claims of over unity in the thermodynamic sense, and perpetual motion must be met with the greatest scepticism, the fact is one can come very close and that is not prohibited by the 1st or second laws. No thermodynamic entropy is not disorder, it is just the natural tendency of energy to move from a concentrated state to a less dense state unless prevented by some barrier like activation energies.  So what is the point I am making. Energy does not die, get used up, it transforms. Also one needs to think about how it transforms locally.  For instance think about the heat energy which goes into the mechanical work of a piston. It is in the gas which has been expanded and in the pressure of that gas, we have to remove the heat to get the cycle.  Well if we have a lower temperature that thermal energy  can flow to and its converted into chemical potential (a thermal battery) then instead of the thermal energy being rejected and lost we have stored it for future use. We have stored the energy internally. As long as ambient temperature conditions remain the same and temperature never rises above ambient we can have local cold temperature zones rising up to ambient. By controlling the path and using entropy one can ensure one never reaches equilibrium for a very long time. A chemical oscillator.  Now is this going to be over unity? NO!  Work will always be a fraction of the heat flow, that is all Carnot efficiency is, its the efficiency of the ratio of heat flow to work, but if one keeps relatively the internal energy constant by regeneration of that energy then one is much more efficient in energy use.  Is this perpetual motion. NO if you are powering by having say a phase change, then you must have that temperature to support the phase change. Meaning that you may not be taking from ambient, but you will lose internal energy if the ambient condition falls.  There are many phenomena which can be regenerated, electrostatic, thermal, etc.  Things is most phenomena output heat as part of their losses.  In some cases in the technical sense you could regen all energy flows to 99.9% but it would be cost prohibitive. Also any device over time is going to degrade. If you use hydrogen or helium in a heat engine you will leak mass.  So the challenge is to design systems which do not lose mass or heat aka internal energy for very long times, and are constrained such that they can never reach equilibrium.  For those who are interested I can take you through such a system step by step and I do invite critics. I can prove it. It's not perpetual nor is it over unity, but to the casual observer it may appear that way. I can move 22,000 KJ of thermal energy locally and produce only 1 KW of power, but I am doing it at 10C as a base temp and the ambient conditions are 10 C. As temp never rises above 10C I cannot lose internal energy to the environment, as at 10C I produce 607 PSi of Hydrogen Pressure, I can use a quarter of the fluid power to generate a local temperature drop but because its local and connected to it is the hydride at ambient and an insulator outside that, what happens is an oscillating temperature profile. Just like you see with this large thermal battery called earth. That is why at a certain depth despite changes in temperature at the surface the temperature is constant.    So as Feynman once said if you want to make a revolution you need to attack the problem from a different angle. Everyone else has tried the well beaten path.  This works within the current laws of thermodynamics it uses chemical kinetics as well as thermodynamics to develop a regenerative energy system.  I have found one path to a highly regenerative energy system, but I know there must be others. Its not free energy but it can be very low cost energy. It's not perpetual but the earth itself and our galaxy is not perpetual.  You can't get more energy than the energy flows but you can recycle.   It easy to say you can't do this, its harder to find a way you can. For the latter means really thinking about it, understanding the problems and constraints at a deep level, and then formulating a solution and testing it.

replies.author: @lstarrtna4288
replies.text: Your clever

replies.author: @chaorrottai
replies.text:
Here's the other thing. The kind of equilibrium described by physics to dis-permit the idea of a perpetual energy harvesting or perpetual energy conversion is a mathematical fantasy. In an actual, real thermal system you don't have a flat distribution of energy, you have an jagged and rough distribution of quantum fluctuations of energy and a constant exchange of energy from one form to another form.

The karpen cell leverages a difference in work function, triboelectric series velocity of ions to cause a biased flow of electrons in an external circuit.

It's ultimately just thermal energy reciprocating into and out of charge separation but it's the difference in the magnitude of charge separation surrounding the boundaries of each electrode that causes the current to flow.


Ultimately all of the energy ends up returning to being heat energy, it's just that something useful to us with done with charges along that reciprocal pathway.

author: @joeglory425
text: Like others I thought this might be an aluminum air battery; and if not designed properly, it certainly would be one--at least to some extent.  I built a cell using US baking soda and for the electrodes I used aluminum foil and carbon fiber "tow".  I sought to eliminate air so I put a layer of vegetable oil on top of the electrolyte. Water based electrolyte of any kind will wick up the carbon fiber.  In the past I've used paraffin wax or epoxy resin on a section of the carbon fiber to block the wicking action--this helps but is not perfect.  Lately I've discovered melted sulfur on the carbon fiber forms a barrier that stops all wicking of the electrolyte up the carbon fiber.  I bring this up both as a useful tool and to highlight it brings a level of uncertainty to my results because the sulfur could be  electrically interacting with the electrodes.  So, I short circuited my experimental cell for about 14 hours to hopefully use up all the dissolved oxygen in the electrolyte (assuming an aluminum air battery).  At the end of this short circuit discharge period the current was 0.03mA.  The size of the electrodes exposed to electrolyte was: aluminum foil size about 3 square inches and the carbon fiber was 3 sections each 1.5 inches long. Only after I did this did I read about the Karpen cell.  The part about using carbon for both electrodes but not being identical, that is being formed from different processes caught my attention.  If one carbon is a N doped semiconductor and the other is a P doped semiconductor and they are separated by an electrolyte why shouldn't it act as a solar collector (in the thermal range of electromagnetic radiation)?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i think you are right there is an element of air battery in this incarnation of the cell - but your most interesting point was the parallel between a solar cell and the karpen cell - very intriguing

replies.author: @joeglory425
replies.text: The cell made from gold and platinum--those are two elements that are both used as catalysts.  What if there's something about the surface of a good catalyst that allows it to harvest ambient energy and then pass it on to other chemicals thereby facilitating the reaction?  If this is the case then you could further generalize to say that a low band gap semiconductor would make a good catalyst. It also means semiconductors with visible light band gaps would also work as a catalyst if the reactants are exposed to light.

author: @jonbigman9723
text:
Well I didn't know what we were doing but that is what we termed the crystalline battery. Adding Alum helps protect the aluminum and adding potassium increases the current to a small degree.. Dang so all along it was a Karpen Cell... Go figure...
 The version you did I tried using aluminum artist wire, coiled around a graphite lead (center). I added Epsom salts for the bulk crystalline structure and for the huge amount of water locked into the Epsom. My averages were 1.25 or so volts new then it dropped to .7 or so steady. After running the battery for over weeks and while the crystals were forming the cell, it seemed to get stronger to about .85 or so volts.
 Washing soda is borax, well at least in the United states. Have fun.

replies.author: @jonbigman9723

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: borax is different it is sodium tetraborate Na2B4O7 - washing soda - in the UK- is sodium carbonate NaCO3 - But I kind of think this is a bit beside the point as what you are saying is much more interesting lol

replies.author: @jonbigman9723
replies.text:
Yeah the crystalline batteries are very very interesting. The premise is to draw from the potential of the electronegative between two bodies of material separated by a crystalline structure and has provisions to protect any metal in the battery. Once the galvanic response has been quashed only the potential difference of the two plates creates the battery. Although the current isn't that high these batteries provide power for extended periods and then must rest to recharge. A different approach would be magnesium and copper type but the more volatile the material the harder it is to protect against oxidation from the liquid state.

 I have seen "crystal" Batteries of alum, potassium and borax in a fresh lead acid battery that can provide some pretty decent current for extended periods. One friend of mine just used borax laundry soap and called it a clean battery. The same friend used oil and water and got excellent results. If you want to know his name it is Ibpointless2 and all of his videos are on the tube.

 In a crystal battery one needs to remember that the standing voltage is not the running potential level. In fact a crystal battery should be designed to have a warm up period where they can settle to their running level with a load. This is usually 1/3 the standing voltage. If you design the battery pack to have 2/3 more standing voltage then the cells should maintain that level for a very long time. With a switching power supply between two banks of batteries you could have an ever lasting battery that should not decay or will but only decay from the usual life of the material.

 What is this energy could be environmental heat energy or the electric field of the earth giving the bodies this standing voltage we call electronegative.
